title: Are LLMs Better than Reported? Detecting Label Errors and Mitigating Their Effect on Model Performance
abstract: NLP benchmarks rely on standardized datasets for training and evaluating models and are crucial for advancing the field. Traditionally, expert annotations ensure high-quality labels; however, the cost of expert annotation does not scale well with the growing demand for larger datasets required by modern models. While crowd-sourcing provides a more scalable solution, it often comes at the expense of annotation precision and consistency. Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) offer new opportunities to enhance the annotation process, particularly for detecting label errors in existing datasets. In this work, we consider the recent approach of LLM-as-a-judge, leveraging an ensemble of LLMs to flag potentially mislabeled examples. We conduct a case study on four factual consistency datasets from the TRUE benchmark, spanning diverse NLP tasks, and on SummEval, which uses Likert-scale ratings of summary quality across multiple dimensions. We empirically analyze the labeling quality of existing datasets and compare expert, crowd-sourced, and LLM-based annotations in terms of the agreement, label quality, and efficiency, demonstrating the strengths and limitations of each annotation method. Our findings reveal a substantial number of label errors, which, when corrected, induce a significant upward shift in reported model performance. This suggests that many of the LLMs' so-called mistakes are due to label errors rather than genuine model failures. Additionally, we discuss the implications of mislabeled data and propose methods to mitigate them in training to improve performance.

title: Transition Function Prediction in AI Planning Using LLMs
venue: AAAI 2025 Workshop on Language Models for Planning (LM4Plan)
abstract: Classical AI planning requires an explicit transition function describing how actions change the environment. Constructing these transition models is often labor-intensive and requires domain expertise. In this work, we investigate whether large language models can predict transition functions directly from natural language descriptions of planning domains. We evaluate LLMs on their ability to infer action effects and state transitions across a range of planning tasks. Our results show that LLMs can often generate plausible transition models, suggesting a promising direction for integrating language models into planning pipelines. At the same time, we identify systematic limitations and discuss challenges in reliability and correctness when using LLM-generated transition functions for downstream planning tasks.

title: Decongestion by Representation: Learning to Improve Economic Welfare in Marketplaces
abstract: Many marketplaces suffer from congestion: when too many users select the same resources, overall welfare decreases. In this work, we introduce Decongestion by Representation, a learning framework that improves economic welfare by shaping the representation used by decision-making agents. Rather than modifying incentives or imposing coordination mechanisms, the approach modifies the information representation provided to agents so that individually rational decisions lead to better collective outcomes. Experiments in simulated marketplace environments demonstrate that the learned representations significantly reduce congestion and increase overall welfare while preserving agents' ability to optimize their own objectives.
